pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: The clinical team focused heavily on managing the psychosis, and the patient was started on a course of Phenytoin for presumed non-convulsive status epilepticus before the neuropathy became severe. Phenytoin (and the preceding severe hyponatremia) served as the catastrophic metabolic trigger. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: AIP is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a partial deficiency of the hepatic enzyme Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase (HMBS), formerly known as Porphobilinogen deaminase (PBG-D). When the enzyme is stressed (e.g., by fasting, certain drugs, infection, or hormonal changes), the synthesis pathway stalls. This leads to the massive accumulation of porphyrin precursors: Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-Aminolevulinic Acid (ALA). ALA and PBG are neurotoxic. The neurological symptoms (psychosis, autonomic instability, and neuropathy) are believed to be caused by direct toxicity on the central, autonomic, and peripheral nervous systems, particularly causing demyelination and axonal damage in the motor nerves (hence the wrist/foot drop). The abdominal pain is due to autonomic neuropathy affecting the gut, not inflammation, which explains the lack of physical signs or radiographic evidence. Diagnosis Confirmation: Elevated urine ALA and PBG levels, which would be dramatically high during the acute attack. Treatment Strategy: Immediate cessation of all precipitating drugs, high-dose intravenous dextrose infusion (to suppress hepatic heme synthesis), and administration of intravenous Heme Arginate (or Hematin), which acts as feedback inhibition to shut down the upstream enzyme ALAS1, halting the production of the toxic precursors.
